_meta.description: Food business permits and regulations from MAPAQ (Ministère de l'Agriculture, des Pêcheries et de l'Alimentation du Québec). Covers home kitchen businesses, market vendors, cottage food operations.

plain_language_summary: If you prepare or sell food in Quebec — whether from your home kitchen, at a farmers' market, or in a commercial space — you need authorization from MAPAQ (the provincial food safety ministry). The type of authorization depends on what you make, where you make it, and how much you sell. Home bakers, pastry chefs, jam makers, and market food vendors all fall under this.

permit_types.plain_language: Quebec allows home kitchen food businesses under specific conditions without a full MAPAQ permit. This is the most common starting point for home bakers.

who_qualifies: Selling baked goods (bread, pastries, cakes, cookies) made in your home kitchen
Selling jams, jellies, preserves (high-acid or high-sugar products)
Selling certain dried goods and confectionery
Annual revenue under a certain threshold (verify current limit with MAPAQ)
Selling directly to end consumers (markets, online, from home) — NOT to retailers or restaurants
who_does_not_qualify: Selling meat, poultry, or seafood products
Selling dairy products or anything requiring refrigeration for safety
Selling to grocery stores, restaurants, or other businesses (requires commercial kitchen)
Annual revenue exceeding MAPAQ threshold (verify current amount)
Products requiring special handling (low-acid canned goods, sprouts)
eligibility.future_eligibility: If you're currently selling to retailers and don't qualify for the exemption, you can qualify by renting a certified commercial kitchen for production.

requirements: Complete MAPAQ's free online food safety training (Formation en hygiène et salubrité alimentaires)
Keep your home kitchen clean and separate from non-food preparation areas
Label products with: your name, address, ingredient list, allergen declarations, production date
Do not make products that require refrigeration for food safety

cost.note: Free — no permit fee, free training

permit_types.timeline: Training takes approximately 3 hours online. No waiting period after completing training.

permit_types.plain_language: Required if you're operating a commercial kitchen, food truck, catering business, or selling products that don't qualify for the home kitchen exemption.

who_qualifies: Any food business not covered by the home kitchen exemption
Businesses selling to retailers or restaurants

requirements: Complete MAPAQ food safety training (mandatory for permit holder)
Commercial kitchen meeting MAPAQ standards (inspected before permit issued)
Submit permit application to MAPAQ with business details

cost.note: Verify exact fee for your establishment type at MAPAQ website

permit_types.timeline: 4-8 weeks from application to permit issuance. Inspection must happen before permit is granted.

food_safety_training.name: Formation en hygiène et salubrité alimentaires
food_safety_training.mandatory_for: Anyone who handles food commercially in Quebec (required for permit holder, strongly recommended for all staff)

mandatory_on_all_food_products: Your name and address (for home kitchen products)
Complete ingredient list (in descending order by weight)
Allergen declarations (the 14 priority allergens must be declared)
labelling_requirements.language_requirement: Labels must be in French (Bill 96). English may be added but French must be at least as prominent.

market_vendor_rules.plain_language: If you sell at farmers' markets in Quebec, each market may have its own rules, but you must still comply with MAPAQ regulations.
requirements: MAPAQ authorization (home kitchen exemption or full permit)
Some markets require proof of insurance — check with specific market
Marché Jean-Talon, Marché Atwater: contact market administration for vendor requirements

montreal_markets.name: Marché Jean-Talon
montreal_markets.note: Contact market for vendor application — waitlists may apply

prerequisites.required_before: Applying for any permit or starting food sales
Applying for food establishment permit (not required for home kitchen exemption)

common_mistakes: Selling at markets without any MAPAQ authorization — inspectors do visit markets
Making products that require refrigeration (cream-filled pastries, cheesecakes) under the home kitchen exemption — these require a full permit
Not declaring allergens on labels — can result in product recall and fines
Labelling in English only — French labelling is mandatory under Bill 96
Thinking the home kitchen exemption covers selling to restaurants or cafés — it doesn't
Not renewing the food safety training certificate every 5 years

enforcement.who_enforces: MAPAQ inspectors, sometimes in conjunction with municipal inspectors
enforcement.inspections: Unannounced for commercial kitchens. Market inspections happen during market season.
enforcement.penalties: Fines ranging from $500 to $62,500 for violations under the Food Products Act

government_body: Ministère de l'Agriculture, des Pêcheries et de l'Alimentation du Québec (MAPAQ)
